title: Raised Intracranial Pressure
oneLiner: A life-threatening condition where brain swelling, mass, or CSF obstruction increases pressure inside the skull—can herniate and kill in minutes.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Brain herniation (transtentorial/foramen magnum):** Look for **Cushing’s triad** (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular respirations) + **unilateral fixed dilated pupil** — this is the sign that herniation is happening now. Urgent neurosurgery + mannitol / hypertonic saline.
- **Seizure (post-ictal states can mimic raised ICP):** Look for **tonic-clonic movements, tongue bite, incontinence** — but note: prolonged tonic-clonic seizure can itself cause raised ICP. Check glucose (hypoglycaemia), bloods, and EEG if confused.
- **Acute hydrocephalus:** Sudden collapse + **“setting sun” eyes** or rapid GCS drop. Needs immediate VP shunt or external ventricular drain (EVD) — not just mannitol.
sections.Presentation:
- **Symptoms:**
  - Headache (worse on lying down, coughing, sneezing — “exertional”)
  - Nausea / vomiting (often early morning, without nausea)
  - Blurred vision / diplopia (CN VI palsy)
  - GCS drop / drowsiness / confusion
- **Exam findings:**
  - Papilloedema (optic disc swelling on fundoscopy — not always present acutely)
  - Abducens nerve palsy (CN VI — false localising sign)
  - Cushing’s triad (late sign)
  - Hypertension + bradycardia
  - Irregular breathing pattern (Cheyne-Stokes, apneustic, or ataxic)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Intracranial mass (tumour, abscess):** Focal neurological symptoms, gradual onset, seizures.
  - **Haemorrhage (SAH, ICH, SDH):** Thunderclap headache (SAH) or focal deficit + signs of mass effect.
  - **Hydrocephalus:** Gait instability, urinary incontinence, cognitive decline (classic triad — but often incomplete).
  - **Cerebral oedema (e.g. DKA, hepatic encephalopathy, traumatic brain injury):** Known precipitant, no focal signs early.
  - **Venous sinus thrombosis:** Headache + papilloedema, sometimes seizures, raised ICP without mass.
  - **Benign intracranial hypertension (IIH):** Obese young female, normal imaging, high opening pressure on LP.
- **Splitter:** The single best differentiator between **mass lesion** and **diffuse oedema** is **CT head** — mass shows midline shift or effacement; oedema shows diffuse sulcal effacement without shift.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Pupillary light reflex (size, symmetry, reactivity)
  - GCS every 15 minutes if unstable
  - Blood glucose (finger-prick) — exclude hypoglycaemia
  - Fundoscopy (if possible) — papilloedema
  - Capillary refill / BP / HR / SpO₂ — any hypotension worsens ICP
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, clotting screen (PT/APTT/INR)
  - CRP (suspect infection/abscess)
  - Blood culture if fever
  - Serum osmolality (before giving mannitol)
  - Drug screen (if overdose suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT head (non-contrast) — look for mass, bleed, hydrocephalus, shift, effacement
  - **Second-line:** CT venogram (if suspect venous sinus thrombosis) or MRI brain (for better parenchymal detail)
- **Special tests:**
  - Lumbar puncture — **only after CT has excluded mass lesion** (risk of herniation). Measure opening pressure (normal 10–20 cm H₂O)
  - EEG if seizures suspected
- **Never-miss test:** **CT head before LP** — performing LP without CT in suspected raised ICP can cause fatal herniation.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **ABC:** Secure airway if GCS ≤8 → intubate (controlled ventilation: PaCO₂ ~4.5 kPa — avoid hyperventilation)
  - **Position:** Head of bed elevated to 30° — improves venous drainage
  - **IV access x 2** — fluid resuscitate with isotonic fluids only (0.9% saline, NOT hypotonic)
  - **Mannitol:** 0.25–1 g/kg IV over 20 min (or hypertonic saline 3% 2 mL/kg) — [check local policy] for dose
  - **Neurosurgery referral immediately** if mass effect, hydrocephalus, or herniation signs
  - **Seizure control:** If seizing, give lorazepam 4 mg IV (or midazolam 10 mg buccal) — then consider levetiracetam loading dose
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Monitoring:** hourly GCS, pupillary check, NEWS2
  - **Oxygen:** target SpO₂ ≥94%
  - **Avoid:** coughing, Valsalva manoeuvres, straining (stool softeners if constipated)
  - **Analgesia:** paracetamol, avoid opioids if possible (depress respiration)
  - **Antibiotics:** if suspect CNS infection (e.g. ceftriaxone + benzylpenicillin + metronidazole) — [check local guidelines]
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Depends on cause — tumour resection, VP shunt for hydrocephalus, weight loss for IIH
  - Neurorehabilitation if significant deficit
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride at 1 mL/kg/hour (avoid hypotonic fluids like 0.45% saline or dextrose)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (if no active bleeding or coagulopathy)
  - **Diet:** NBM if GCS low or risk of aspiration; otherwise normal diet but limit excessive fluids
  - **Observations:** GCS + pupil check every hour for first 24h, then 4-hourly if stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line for acute ICP reduction:**
  - Mannitol 20%: 0.25–1 g/kg IV over 20 minutes — [check local policy for exact dose]
  - Hypertonic saline 3%: 2 mL/kg IV bolus — [check local policy]
- **Corticosteroids for vasogenic oedema (tumour, abscess):**
  - Dexamethasone: 10 mg IV stat then 4 mg IV/PO QDS — reduce if improvement
  - **Key contraindication:** Do NOT give if suspect cerebral oedema from infection (e.g. TB, fungal) — can worsen
- **Seizure prophylaxis:**
  - Levetiracetam: 500 mg–1 g IV bid (if needed)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Mannitol and hypertonic saline require normal renal function (risk of AKI). Dexamethasone no dose adjustment in renal failure.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Mannitol given without checking serum osmolality** — can cause hyperosmolar state and AKI. Also: **Over-enthusiastic hyperventilation** (PaCO₂ <4 kPa) causing cerebral ischaemia.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Brain herniation (fatal within minutes)
  - Hydrocephalus
  - Seizures
  - Cushing’s ulcer (gastric stress ulcer) — consider PPI prophylaxis
  - Hypotension (if over-diuresed)
- **Chronic:**
  - Cognitive impairment
  - Visual loss (optic atrophy)
  - Permanent neurological deficit
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality is high without treatment (~50% in acute severe cases). With prompt intervention, depends on cause—mass lesions have better outcomes if resected early. Chronic mild raised ICP (e.g. IIH) rarely fatal but visual loss common without treatment.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always do a CT head before LP** in any patient with headache + focal signs or papillodoema — you will save a life.
- **Elevate the head of bed to 30°** — simple, immediate, and effective for reducing ICP.
- **Give dexamethasone 10 mg IV stat** if vasogenic oedema from tumour is suspected — even before imaging is reported (if no contraindication).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give dextrose or hypotonic fluids** — they lower serum osmolality and worsen cerebral oedema.
- **Do not give mannitol without checking renal function** — risk of acute tubular necrosis.
- **Do not give steroids in suspected CNS infection** — can mask signs and worsen prognosis (especially TB).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** (rarely appropriate for raised ICP — most are admitted)
  - New headache worse than before, especially with vomiting or visual change
  - Any new weakness, speech difficulty, or seizure
  - Worsening confusion or drowsiness
- **Follow-up:** Urgent neurosurgery OPA within 1–2 weeks if not admitted; MRI brain if not yet done.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU immediately if:**
  - GCS drop by ≥2 points
  - Pupil becomes fixed and dilated (unilateral or bilateral)
  - Cushing’s triad (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular respiration)
  - NEWS2 ≥7
  - Lactate ≥4 mmol/L
  - Any signs of herniation (posturing, irregular breathing)
- **Crash call if:** cardiac arrest or respiratory arrest.
- **Neurosurgical emergency:** any acute hydrocephalus or mass effect requiring decompression — transfer to neurosurgical centre.

rawMarkdown:
## Raised Intracranial Pressure

**One-liner:** A life-threatening condition where brain swelling, mass, or CSF obstruction increases pressure inside the skull—can herniate and kill in minutes.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Brain herniation (transtentorial/foramen magnum):** Look for **Cushing’s triad** (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular respirations) + **unilateral fixed dilated pupil** — this is the sign that herniation is happening now. Urgent neurosurgery + mannitol / hypertonic saline.
- **Seizure (post-ictal states can mimic raised ICP):** Look for **tonic-clonic movements, tongue bite, incontinence** — but note: prolonged tonic-clonic seizure can itself cause raised ICP. Check glucose (hypoglycaemia), bloods, and EEG if confused.
- **Acute hydrocephalus:** Sudden collapse + **“setting sun” eyes** or rapid GCS drop. Needs immediate VP shunt or external ventricular drain (EVD) — not just mannitol.

### Presentation

- **Symptoms:**
  - Headache (worse on lying down, coughing, sneezing — “exertional”)
  - Nausea / vomiting (often early morning, without nausea)
  - Blurred vision / diplopia (CN VI palsy)
  - GCS drop / drowsiness / confusion
- **Exam findings:**
  - Papilloedema (optic disc swelling on fundoscopy — not always present acutely)
  - Abducens nerve palsy (CN VI — false localising sign)
  - Cushing’s triad (late sign)
  - Hypertension + bradycardia
  - Irregular breathing pattern (Cheyne-Stokes, apneustic, or ataxic)

### Differential Diagnosis

- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Intracranial mass (tumour, abscess):** Focal neurological symptoms, gradual onset, seizures.
  - **Haemorrhage (SAH, ICH, SDH):** Thunderclap headache (SAH) or focal deficit + signs of mass effect.
  - **Hydrocephalus:** Gait instability, urinary incontinence, cognitive decline (classic triad — but often incomplete).
  - **Cerebral oedema (e.g. DKA, hepatic encephalopathy, traumatic brain injury):** Known precipitant, no focal signs early.
  - **Venous sinus thrombosis:** Headache + papilloedema, sometimes seizures, raised ICP without mass.
  - **Benign intracranial hypertension (IIH):** Obese young female, normal imaging, high opening pressure on LP.
- **Splitter:** The single best differentiator between **mass lesion** and **diffuse oedema** is **CT head** — mass shows midline shift or effacement; oedema shows diffuse sulcal effacement without shift.

### Investigations

- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Pupillary light reflex (size, symmetry, reactivity)
  - GCS every 15 minutes if unstable
  - Blood glucose (finger-prick) — exclude hypoglycaemia
  - Fundoscopy (if possible) — papilloedema
  - Capillary refill / BP / HR / SpO₂ — any hypotension worsens ICP
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, clotting screen (PT/APTT/INR)
  - CRP (suspect infection/abscess)
  - Blood culture if fever
  - Serum osmolality (before giving mannitol)
  - Drug screen (if overdose suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT head (non-contrast) — look for mass, bleed, hydrocephalus, shift, effacement
  - **Second-line:** CT venogram (if suspect venous sinus thrombosis) or MRI brain (for better parenchymal detail)
- **Special tests:**
  - Lumbar puncture — **only after CT has excluded mass lesion** (risk of herniation). Measure opening pressure (normal 10–20 cm H₂O)
  - EEG if seizures suspected
- **Never-miss test:** **CT head before LP** — performing LP without CT in suspected raised ICP can cause fatal herniation.

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **ABC:** Secure airway if GCS ≤8 → intubate (controlled ventilation: PaCO₂ ~4.5 kPa — avoid hyperventilation)
  - **Position:** Head of bed elevated to 30° — improves venous drainage
  - **IV access x 2** — fluid resuscitate with isotonic fluids only (0.9% saline, NOT hypotonic)
  - **Mannitol:** 0.25–1 g/kg IV over 20 min (or hypertonic saline 3% 2 mL/kg) — [check local policy] for dose
  - **Neurosurgery referral immediately** if mass effect, hydrocephalus, or herniation signs
  - **Seizure control:** If seizing, give lorazepam 4 mg IV (or midazolam 10 mg buccal) — then consider levetiracetam loading dose
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Monitoring:** hourly GCS, pupillary check, NEWS2
  - **Oxygen:** target SpO₂ ≥94%
  - **Avoid:** coughing, Valsalva manoeuvres, straining (stool softeners if constipated)
  - **Analgesia:** paracetamol, avoid opioids if possible (depress respiration)
  - **Antibiotics:** if suspect CNS infection (e.g. ceftriaxone + benzylpenicillin + metronidazole) — [check local guidelines]
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Depends on cause — tumour resection, VP shunt for hydrocephalus, weight loss for IIH
  - Neurorehabilitation if significant deficit
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride at 1 mL/kg/hour (avoid hypotonic fluids like 0.45% saline or dextrose)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (if no active bleeding or coagulopathy)
  - **Diet:** NBM if GCS low or risk of aspiration; otherwise normal diet but limit excessive fluids
  - **Observations:** GCS + pupil check every hour for first 24h, then 4-hourly if stable

### Drugs & Doses

- **First-line for acute ICP reduction:**
  - Mannitol 20%: 0.25–1 g/kg IV over 20 minutes — [check local policy for exact dose]
  - Hypertonic saline 3%: 2 mL/kg IV bolus — [check local policy]
- **Corticosteroids for vasogenic oedema (tumour, abscess):**
  - Dexamethasone: 10 mg IV stat then 4 mg IV/PO QDS — reduce if improvement
  - **Key contraindication:** Do NOT give if suspect cerebral oedema from infection (e.g. TB, fungal) — can worsen
- **Seizure prophylaxis:**
  - Levetiracetam: 500 mg–1 g IV bid (if needed)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Mannitol and hypertonic saline require normal renal function (risk of AKI). Dexamethasone no dose adjustment in renal failure.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Mannitol given without checking serum osmolality** — can cause hyperosmolar state and AKI. Also: **Over-enthusiastic hyperventilation** (PaCO₂ <4 kPa) causing cerebral ischaemia.

### Complications

- **Acute:**
  - Brain herniation (fatal within minutes)
  - Hydrocephalus
  - Seizures
  - Cushing’s ulcer (gastric stress ulcer) — consider PPI prophylaxis
  - Hypotension (if over-diuresed)
- **Chronic:**
  - Cognitive impairment
  - Visual loss (optic atrophy)
  - Permanent neurological deficit

### Prognosis

- Mortality is high without treatment (~50% in acute severe cases). With prompt intervention, depends on cause—mass lesions have better outcomes if resected early. Chronic mild raised ICP (e.g. IIH) rarely fatal but visual loss common without treatment.

### Pearls (positive actions)

- **Always do a CT head before LP** in any patient with headache + focal signs or papillodoema — you will save a life.
- **Elevate the head of bed to 30°** — simple, immediate, and effective for reducing ICP.
- **Give dexamethasone 10 mg IV stat** if vasogenic oedema from tumour is suspected — even before imaging is reported (if no contraindication).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

- **Do not give dextrose or hypotonic fluids** — they lower serum osmolality and worsen cerebral oedema.
- **Do not give mannitol without checking renal function** — risk of acute tubular necrosis.
- **Do not give steroids in suspected CNS infection** — can mask signs and worsen prognosis (especially TB).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

- **Return if:** (rarely appropriate for raised ICP — most are admitted)
  - New headache worse than before, especially with vomiting or visual change
  - Any new weakness, speech difficulty, or seizure
  - Worsening confusion or drowsiness
- **Follow-up:** Urgent neurosurgery OPA within 1–2 weeks if not admitted; MRI brain if not yet done.

### Escalation Criteria

- **Call senior / ICU immediately if:**
  - GCS drop by ≥2 points
  - Pupil becomes fixed and dilated (unilateral or bilateral)
  - Cushing’s triad (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular respiration)
  - NEWS2 ≥7
  - Lactate ≥4 mmol/L
  - Any signs of herniation (posturing, irregular breathing)
- **Crash call if:** cardiac arrest or respiratory arrest.
- **Neurosurgical emergency:** any acute hydrocephalus or mass effect requiring decompression — transfer to neurosurgical centre.
